turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion this morning in case 15-1262, Cooper versus Harris.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: The constitution's equal protection clause limits how race can be used in legislative districting. The general rule is that a state may not use race as the main consideration in drawing district lines unless it has a compelling reason for doing so. In this case, we apply that rule to two congressional districts in North Carolina. In 2011, North Carolina redrew both those districts, District 1 and District 12 to give the majority black voting populations. Two registered voters filed a lawsuit alleging that the state drew the district boundaries based on race in violation of the constitution. After a trial, a three-judge district court held both districts unconstitutional. Today, we affirm that decision. Start with District 1, the easy question here is whether race was the main reason for drawing District 1 in the way the state did. It was. The state purposely established a target of more than 50% African-American voters and attaining that target was crucially important thing for the state's map-makers. They did all kinds of things they wouldn't otherwise have done to meet their racial goal. And in so doing, they created a district with stark racial borders. The harder question is whether North Carolina had a good enough reason for drawing its lines based on race. North Carolina says it did. We have always assumed that a state can draw race baselines if it needs to do so to comply with the Voting Rights Act. A federal statute designed to protect minority voting rights which I am going to call the VRA. The VRA says that states cannot dilute the influence of minority voters by spreading them out over multiple districts in a way that undercuts their overall electoral clout. North Carolina argues that it would have violated that rule unless it made District 1 what is called a majority-minority district. But in our view the state did not have a sound basis for thinking that the VRA required it to pack more of African-American voters into District 1. Even when District 1 had only a substantial minority of black voters, those voters were already electing their preferred candidates in every election and often in landslides and when that is true, when a district functions as a so-called crossover district with white voters crossing over to make a common cause with black voters, the VRA does not require further increase in the share of black voters. So the state had no good reason for doing so. District 12 presents a different kind of issue. Here, the only question is whether North Carolina drew the district lines based on race or instead based on politics. The state says it was all about politics. It was not trying to pack District 12 with African-American voters the state claims. It was only trying to pack the district with Democrats so that other neighboring districts would be more Republican. The new voters may have been disproportionately black, the state says, but that was just an accidental byproduct of partisan gerrymandering. And under current law, courts do not get involved in partisan gerrymandering. The district court considered a lot of evidence on both sides of that question including testimony by map-makers and legislators, contemporaneous documents explaining what they were doing and statistical analysis. It ultimately found as a matter of fact that the state did make key decisions based on race, not politics. This Court reviews that kind of factual determination only for clear error. As long as the district court's finding was plausible, we uphold it and we think the finding was plausible in light of all the evidence. The state also argues that the court below made a legal mistake in not requiring the people challenging the district to present a particular kind of evidence. A map showing that the state could have satisfied all its partisan objectives without so much racial imbalance but we reject that view. The plaintiffs of course, have the burden to prove race based districting but they can satisfy that burden in whatever way they choose. They do not have to present any particular form of evidence. Accordingly, we also affirm the district court's decision, that District 12 was unconstitutional. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Alito has filed an opinion concurring and the judgment in part and dissenting in part in which the Chief Justice and Justice Kennedy have joined. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
